title: Make a Simple Resume
summary: Create a one-page resume that presents your contact info, experience, skills, and education clearly.
learnerPromise: You will be able to create a clean, one-page resume that shows an employer what you can offer, even if you have little or no formal work experience.
whyItMatters: A resume is often the first thing an employer sees about you. A clear, simple one helps you stand out and can open doors to interviews.
realLifeUses: Attaching a resume to an online job application
Handing a printed resume to a manager when asking about openings
Updating your profile on a job-search website
Sending a resume to a teacher or mentor who offered to help you find work
materialsNeeded: Computer or phone with a word processor or resume app
Your work, volunteer, or school activity history
result: A one-page resume saved as a PDF or printed on clean paper.
youWillLearn: How to structure a resume with clear sections: contact info, experience, skills, and education
How to describe experience using action words, even from informal or volunteer work
How to choose a simple, readable format that works on screens and paper
How to save and share your resume as a PDF so formatting stays consistent
miniChallenge: Create a one-page resume for yourself using a simple template. Include at least one experience entry and three skills, then save it as a PDF.
steps: Open a blank document or simple resume template. Choose a clean, easy-to-read font.
Add your contact information at the top: full name, phone number, professional email address, and city or neighborhood.
List your experience section with entries that include the job title or role, the organization, dates, and 1-2 bullet points about what you did.
Add a skills section with 3-6 abilities relevant to the kind of work you want, such as customer service, organization, or basic computer skills.
Review for spelling and accuracy, then save or export the document as a PDF with a clear file name like 'YourName_Resume.pdf'.
completionCriteria: Your resume fits on one page and includes your contact info, at least one experience entry, and a skills section
The resume is saved as a PDF or printed cleanly with no formatting errors
commonProblems: Trying to make the resume fancy with colors or graphics — simplicity is usually better for entry-level jobs
Including personal details like age, photo, or marital status, which are not needed in most countries
Using an unprofessional email address — create a simple one with your name if needed
Forgetting to proofread, which can leave typos that create a poor impression
tips: Volunteer work, school projects, and helping family businesses all count as experience
Use action words like 'helped,' 'organized,' 'supported,' or 'managed' to describe what you did
Ask someone you trust to read your resume and give feedback before sending it out
Update your resume every time you gain a new skill or experience so it stays current
